essentials.heading: VPC, Subnets, CIDR & AZs
essentials.body: A **VPC** is a logically isolated virtual network scoped to **one Region**, defined by an IPv4 CIDR block (e.g. `10.0.0.0/16`, allowed sizes `/16`–`/28`). Each **subnet** lives in exactly **one Availability Zone** and cannot span AZs, so build at least one subnet per AZ for high availability. A subnet is *public* when its route table sends `0.0.0.0/0` to an **Internet Gateway**; otherwise it is *private*. AWS reserves **5 IP addresses** in every subnet (first four + last), so a `/28` yields only 11 usable hosts.

essentials.heading: Route Tables, IGW, NAT Gateway vs NAT Instance
essentials.body: **Route tables** control traffic per subnet; the *main* route table applies to any subnet without an explicit association. An **Internet Gateway (IGW)** gives public subnets bidirectional internet access. A **NAT Gateway** lets *private* subnet instances make outbound connections while blocking inbound — it is AWS-managed, scales automatically, and is **AZ-scoped** (deploy one per AZ for resilience). A **NAT instance** is a self-managed EC2 alternative (cheaper, but you patch it, manage failover, and must disable *source/destination check*). For **IPv6**, outbound-only access uses an **egress-only Internet Gateway** instead of NAT.

essentials.heading: Security Groups (stateful) vs Network ACLs (stateless)
essentials.body: **Security Groups** operate at the **ENI/instance** level, are **stateful** (return traffic is automatically allowed), support **allow rules only**, and evaluate all rules together. **Network ACLs** operate at the **subnet** level, are **stateless** (you must allow both inbound *and* outbound, including **ephemeral ports** 1024–65535 for return traffic), support **allow and deny**, and are evaluated by **rule number** (lowest first, first match wins). Classic exam trap: to block a specific malicious IP you need a **NACL deny rule** because SGs cannot deny.

essentials.heading: VPC Endpoints: Gateway vs Interface (PrivateLink)
essentials.body: VPC Endpoints let resources reach AWS services privately without an IGW or NAT. **Gateway endpoints** are free and support **only S3 and DynamoDB**; they work by adding a *prefix-list route* to your route table (no ENI, no DNS change). **Interface endpoints** (**AWS PrivateLink**) create an **ENI with a private IP** in your subnet and support most other services (and your own services); they are billed hourly + per GB. Choosing a Gateway endpoint for S3 keeps backup/data traffic off the NAT Gateway, cutting cost.

essentials.heading: VPC Peering vs Transit Gateway
essentials.body: **VPC Peering** is a 1-to-1 connection between two VPCs (cross-account / cross-Region supported) but is **non-transitive** — if A peers B and B peers C, A still cannot reach C, and CIDRs must not overlap. This scales poorly: *n* VPCs need *n(n-1)/2* peerings. **Transit Gateway (TGW)** is a regional hub that connects thousands of VPCs and on-prem (VPN/DX) with **transitive routing** via route tables, simplifying large hub-and-spoke topologies. Pick TGW when many VPCs must interconnect.

essentials.heading: Hybrid Connectivity: Site-to-Site VPN vs Direct Connect
essentials.body: **Site-to-Site VPN** runs an **IPsec encrypted** tunnel over the public internet — quick to set up, low cost, but variable latency. **Direct Connect (DX)** is a dedicated **private physical** link from your data center to AWS, giving consistent low latency and high throughput, but takes weeks to provision and is **not encrypted by itself**. For private *and* encrypted hybrid traffic, run a **VPN over Direct Connect**. DX can also use a **public VIF** to reach public AWS endpoints privately.

essentials.heading: VPC Flow Logs
essentials.body: **VPC Flow Logs** capture IP traffic *metadata* (source/dest IP, ports, protocol, bytes, **ACCEPT/REJECT**) for a **VPC, subnet, or ENI**, publishing to **CloudWatch Logs, S3, or Kinesis Data Firehose**. They are essential for troubleshooting connectivity (e.g. seeing whether a NACL/SG rejected traffic) and for security auditing. Note: Flow Logs record metadata only — **not packet contents/payload** — and do not log traffic to AWS DNS, DHCP, instance metadata (`169.254.169.254`), or Windows license activation.

labs.title: Create a VPC with a public subnet + IGW + route
labs.intro: Build a `10.0.0.0/16` VPC, a public subnet in one AZ, attach an **Internet Gateway**, and add a default route so instances with public IPs can reach the internet.
labs.cli:
VPC_ID=$(aws ec2 create-vpc --cidr-block 10.0.0.0/16 --query Vpc.VpcId --output text)
aws ec2 create-tags --resources $VPC_ID --tags Key=Name,Value=lab-vpc

SUBNET_ID=$(aws ec2 create-subnet --vpc-id $VPC_ID --cidr-block 10.0.1.0/24 --availability-zone eu-west-1a --query Subnet.SubnetId --output text)
aws ec2 modify-subnet-attribute --subnet-id $SUBNET_ID --map-public-ip-on-launch

IGW_ID=$(aws ec2 create-internet-gateway --query InternetGateway.InternetGatewayId --output text)
aws ec2 attach-internet-gateway --internet-gateway-id $IGW_ID --vpc-id $VPC_ID

RT_ID=$(aws ec2 create-route-table --vpc-id $VPC_ID --query RouteTable.RouteTableId --output text)
aws ec2 create-route --route-table-id $RT_ID --destination-cidr-block 0.0.0.0/0 --gateway-id $IGW_ID
aws ec2 associate-route-table --route-table-id $RT_ID --subnet-id $SUBNET_ID
labs.pulumi:
import pulumi_aws as aws

vpc = aws.ec2.Vpc("lab-vpc", cidr_block="10.0.0.0/16", tags={"Name": "lab-vpc"})

igw = aws.ec2.InternetGateway("lab-igw", vpc_id=vpc.id)

subnet = aws.ec2.Subnet(
    "public-subnet",
    vpc_id=vpc.id,
    cidr_block="10.0.1.0/24",
    availability_zone="eu-west-1a",
    map_public_ip_on_launch=True,
)

rt = aws.ec2.RouteTable(
    "public-rt",
    vpc_id=vpc.id,
    routes=[aws.ec2.RouteTableRouteArgs(cidr_block="0.0.0.0/0", gateway_id=igw.id)],
)

aws.ec2.RouteTableAssociation("public-rta", subnet_id=subnet.id, route_table_id=rt.id)
labs.note: Cleanup (reverse order): disassociate + delete the route table, detach + delete the IGW (`aws ec2 detach-internet-gateway` then `delete-internet-gateway`), delete the subnet, then delete the VPC. With Pulumi just run `pulumi destroy`. A VPC will refuse to delete while any dependent ENI, IGW, subnet, or route table still references it.

labs.title: Create a Gateway VPC Endpoint for S3
labs.intro: Add a **Gateway endpoint** for S3 so private-subnet traffic to S3 stays on the AWS network and avoids the NAT Gateway (free + cheaper). The endpoint injects an S3 *prefix-list* route into the chosen route tables.
labs.cli:
REGION=eu-west-1
aws ec2 create-vpc-endpoint \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --vpc-endpoint-type Gateway \
  --service-name com.amazonaws.$REGION.s3 \
  --route-table-ids $RT_ID \
  --query VpcEndpoint.VpcEndpointId --output text
labs.pulumi:
import pulumi_aws as aws

region = aws.get_region().name

s3_endpoint = aws.ec2.VpcEndpoint(
    "s3-gateway-endpoint",
    vpc_id=vpc.id,
    service_name=f"com.amazonaws.{region}.s3",
    vpc_endpoint_type="Gateway",
    route_table_ids=[rt.id],
)
labs.note: Cleanup: `aws ec2 delete-vpc-endpoint --vpc-endpoint-ids <id>` (or `pulumi destroy`). Gateway endpoints incur no hourly charge, so leaving one briefly is harmless, but deleting it removes the auto-added route. Verify the route appears as a `pl-xxxx` prefix list target in the route table after creation.

questions.stem: A security team must block a single abusive public IP address from reaching all instances in a subnet, while keeping all other traffic flowing. What should they configure?
options: An IGW filter rule for the IP
A Security Group inbound rule that denies the IP
A route table entry blackholing the IP
A Network ACL inbound deny rule for that IP with a lower rule number than the allow rule
questions.explanation: Correct: NACLs are stateless and support **deny** rules evaluated lowest-number-first, so a low-numbered deny for the IP blocks it before a broader allow. Security Groups support **allow rules only** — they cannot deny, so that option is impossible. Route tables route by destination CIDR and cannot selectively block a source IP for filtering purposes. There is no such thing as an 'IGW filter rule'.

questions.stem: Instances in private subnets across three Availability Zones need outbound internet access for OS updates. The architect wants a managed, highly available solution. What is the best approach?
options: Deploy one NAT Gateway per AZ and point each subnet's route table to the NAT Gateway in its own AZ
Deploy a single NAT Gateway and route all three subnets to it
Attach an Internet Gateway and give each instance a public IP
Deploy a single NAT instance with source/destination check enabled
questions.explanation: Correct: NAT Gateways are AZ-scoped, so one per AZ with same-AZ routing avoids a cross-AZ single point of failure and data charges. A single NAT Gateway works but loses access if its AZ fails (not highly available). An IGW with public IPs would make the subnets public and expose instances to inbound traffic. A NAT instance with source/dest check *enabled* would actually drop the forwarded traffic, and a single one isn't HA.

questions.stem: An application in a private subnet performs heavy data transfers to Amazon S3. The team wants to minimize cost and avoid sending this traffic through the NAT Gateway. What should they implement?
options: A second NAT Gateway dedicated to S3 traffic
An Interface VPC endpoint (PrivateLink) for S3
A Gateway VPC endpoint for S3
VPC peering to an AWS-managed S3 VPC
questions.explanation: Correct: A **Gateway endpoint** for S3 is free, adds a prefix-list route, and keeps traffic off the NAT Gateway, cutting data-processing charges. An Interface endpoint for S3 exists but is billed hourly + per GB, so it's not the cheapest for high volume. Adding a NAT Gateway increases cost. You cannot peer to an AWS-managed service VPC for S3 access.

questions.stem: A company has 12 VPCs that all must communicate with each other and with an on-premises data center. They want to avoid managing a full mesh of connections. Which service best fits?
options: VPC Peering between every pair of VPCs
An Internet Gateway shared across VPCs
A separate Site-to-Site VPN between each VPC
AWS Transit Gateway as a central hub
questions.explanation: Correct: Transit Gateway provides transitive, hub-and-spoke routing for many VPCs plus on-prem (via VPN/DX) from a single attachment per VPC. Full-mesh VPC peering would need 66 connections and peering is non-transitive, so it scales poorly. An IGW cannot interconnect VPCs. Per-VPC VPNs don't solve VPC-to-VPC connectivity and multiply management overhead.

questions.stem: A business needs a dedicated, consistent-latency connection from its data center to AWS, but the data crossing the link must also be encrypted in transit. Which combination meets both requirements? (Choose two)
options: Use a Site-to-Site VPN over the public internet only
Rely on Direct Connect alone, which encrypts traffic by default
Provision AWS Direct Connect for the private dedicated link
Run a Site-to-Site IPsec VPN over the Direct Connect connection
questions.explanation: Correct: Direct Connect supplies the dedicated low-latency private link, and layering an IPsec **VPN over DX** adds the required encryption. Direct Connect by itself is **not encrypted**, so relying on it alone fails the encryption requirement. A VPN over the public internet alone provides encryption but not the dedicated consistent-latency link the business needs.

questions.stem: After deploying an instance, an engineer can SSH out but a web service running on it is unreachable from the internet even though the Security Group allows port 443 inbound. The subnet uses a custom Network ACL. What is the most likely cause?
options: The Internet Gateway only supports stateful traffic
The route table is missing a route to the Security Group
The Security Group is blocking the return traffic
The Network ACL is missing an outbound allow rule for ephemeral ports (1024-65535)
questions.explanation: Correct: NACLs are **stateless**, so the response from port 443 leaves via an **ephemeral port** that the outbound NACL must explicitly allow; missing that rule drops the reply. The Security Group is **stateful**, so it automatically allows return traffic and isn't the issue. IGWs don't have a stateful/stateless distinction. Route tables route to gateways/CIDRs, not to Security Groups, so that option is nonsensical.

questions.stem: A security audit requires capturing metadata about all accepted and rejected IP traffic for a specific subnet, to be analyzed later in Athena. Which solution should be used?
options: Enable Security Group logging on each instance
Enable VPC Flow Logs on the subnet, delivering to an S3 bucket
Enable AWS CloudTrail data events for the VPC
Mirror full packet payloads to a third-party appliance
questions.explanation: Correct: VPC Flow Logs capture ACCEPT/REJECT traffic metadata at the subnet level and can deliver to S3, which Athena can query directly. CloudTrail logs API/management activity, not packet-level traffic. Full packet mirroring (Traffic Mirroring) captures payloads but is overkill when only metadata is needed and isn't S3/Athena native. There is no 'Security Group logging' feature.

questions.stem: Instances in a private subnet need outbound-only access to the internet over IPv6 (e.g., to fetch updates) without allowing any inbound connections. Which component provides this?
options: An egress-only Internet Gateway
A NAT instance with an Elastic IP
A NAT Gateway
An Internet Gateway with a public IPv6 route
questions.explanation: Correct: An **egress-only Internet Gateway** is the IPv6 equivalent of NAT, allowing stateful outbound IPv6 connections while blocking unsolicited inbound. NAT Gateways and NAT instances operate on **IPv4** only and do not handle IPv6. A plain IGW with an IPv6 route provides full bidirectional access, which would expose the instances to inbound traffic.
